title: Mince pies
subtitle: Shortcrust pastry cases filled with spiced fruit mincemeat
excerpt: Traditional British Christmas pies made with a rich, short pastry and filled with spiced fruit mincemeat. Crisp base, tender top, fragrant with warm spice. Makes eighteen.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: mince pies documented as a Christmas household standard made with shortcrust pastry and a spiced mincemeat filling. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): mince pies appear in the seasonal household entries with both lard and butter shortcrust variants. The mincemeat, a mixture of dried fruits, spices, and suet, is documented across English household records from the medieval period, where it originally contained meat as well as fruit.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and fully cooled. Reheat from frozen at 180°C fan for 8 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly. Use two tins and bake in batches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry can be made up to 48 hours ahead and refrigerated. The assembled unbaked pies can be frozen on a tray, then transferred to a bag and baked from frozen.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 210°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: A spiced mixture of dried fruits, citrus peel, sugar, suet, and spirits used as a filling for mince pies. Originally contained actual minced meat alongside the fruit — the combination of sweet dried fruit with savoury meat was standard in medieval and Tudor cooking. Modern mincemeat contains no meat; the name is purely historical. Jars keep unopened for over a year; once opened, use within a month.

content.text: The shortcrust for mince pies should be richer and shorter than standard shortcrust. Icing sugar rather than caster gives a more tender, less crumbly pastry that holds its shape when cut into rounds. Use a lard-and-butter blend if you like: lard gives a shorter, crisper pastry; butter gives flavour. The
filling can come from a jar.

Rub the cold {{unsalted-butter}} into the {{plain-flour}} and {{icing-sugar}} (pastry amount) until the mixture resembles fine crumbs. Add half the beaten {{eggs}} and 1–2 tbsp cold water, and press together until a smooth dough forms. Wrap and chill for 30 minutes.

Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Roll the pastry to 3 mm thick. Cut 18 rounds using a 9 cm cutter and press into the holes of a greased bun tin. Place a heaped teaspoon of {{mincemeat}} in each. Cut 18 smaller rounds for the lids and press lightly to seal. Brush with the remaining beaten egg. Cut a small steam hole in each lid. Bake for 18–20 minutes, until pale gold and the pastry is set. Leave to cool in the tin for 5 minutes, then ease out and dust with {{icing-sugar}} (dusting amount). Serve warm or at room temperature.
